title: Assessing the social factors affecting solar energy transition in the mining sector
abstract: This study examines the social factors shaping solar energy transitioning in Zambia’s mining sector, a key contributor to the national resource economy. It focuses on how policies, incentive systems, communication channels, training and skills development, and stakeholder engagement and partnership influence the sector’s readiness to adopt solar energy and support more reliable operations. A quantitative design was used, with a 5-point Likert-scale questionnaire administered to 192 respondents from mining companies, regulatory bodies, energy suppliers and local authorities. Data were analysed using descriptive statistics, reliability tests and hierarchical regression. The results show that stakeholder engagement and partnership and incentive systems are the strongest predictors of transition intentions, underscoring their role in improving energy security and supporting sustainable production. Policies, communication and training had weaker effects, indicating the need for stronger institutional coordination and targeted capacity building. Overall, the findings illustrate how social conditions shape renewable energy adoption and contribute to more resilient mining activities.

Abstract
This study examines the social factors shaping solar energy transitioning in Zambia’s
mining sector, a key contributor to the national resource economy. It focuses on how
policies, incentive systems, communication channels, training and skills development,
and stakeholder engagement and partnership influence the sector’s readiness to adopt
solar energy and support more reliable operations. A quantitative design was used, with
a 5-point Likert-scale questionnaire administered to 192 respondents from mining com-
panies, regulatory bodies, energy suppliers and local authorities. Data were analysed
using descriptive statistics, reliability tests and hierarchical regression. The results show
that stakeholder engagement and partnership and incentive systems are the strongest
predictors of transition intentions, underscoring their role in improving energy security
and supporting sustainable production. Policies, communication and training had weaker
effects, indicating the need for stronger institutional coordination and targeted capacity
building. Overall, the findings illustrate how social conditions shape renewable energy
adoption and contribute to more resilient mining activities.
Keywords: social factors; solar energy; energy transition; mining sector; Zambia
1. Introduction
Meeting rising global energy demand while reducing dependence on unsustainable
energy sources remains a central challenge of the twenty-first century. In response, countries
are increasingly encouraged to diversify their energy mixes, strengthen the use of local
renewable resources and adopt innovative practices that enhance energy security [1]. This
transition is reinforced by Sustainable Development Goal 7, which promotes universal
access to affordable, reliable and clean energy through renewable technologies such as solar,
wind and thermal energy [1,2]. Achieving this goal is particularly critical for developing
countries such as Zambia, where energy poverty, climate vulnerability and constrained
infrastructure pose persistent development risks [3–5].
Zambia’s energy sector reflects these pressures acutely [6–9]. Rapid population growth,
projected to reach 26.9 million by 2035, is expected to intensify electricity demand across all
sectors [7,10]. At the same time, the country’s heavy reliance on hydropower exposes the
system to increasing risks associated with drought, climate variability, ageing infrastructure

and maintenance backlogs [11]. Within this context, solar energy offers a viable alterna-
tive due to Zambia’s high solar potential, modular scalability and declining technology
costs [12,13]. However, evidence from energy transition studies consistently shows that
technological availability alone is insufficient to drive adoption. Social and institutional
conditions, including policy support, incentive structures, institutional coordination and
stakeholder acceptance, play a decisive role in shaping transition outcomes [5,14–16].
These dynamics are particularly pronounced in Zambia’s mining sector, where re-
newable energy uptake remains limited despite growing energy constraints [17–20]. As
the country’s largest electricity consumer and a central driver of economic growth, the
mining industry plays a critical role in shaping national energy demand and sustainability
outcomes [21–23]. Reliable and affordable electricity supply is essential for continuous
mineral production, cost control and operational stability. Recurrent power shortages and
hydropower disruptions increase production risks and undermine sector performance. So-
lar energy presents an opportunity to improve energy reliability and enhance the resilience
of mining operations; however, progress depends heavily on social conditions such as clear
policy signals, accessible incentives, effective communication mechanisms and coordinated
stakeholder engagement. Understanding how these social factors influence organisational
decision-making is therefore essential for aligning industrial energy practices with national
transition objectives and strengthening the long-term sustainability of the mining sector.
Against this background, this study investigates the social factors affecting solar energy
transitioning in Zambia’s mining sector. Specifically, it examines the relative influence
of policies, incentive systems, communication channels, training and skills development,
and stakeholder engagement and partnerships on organisational intentions to adopt solar
energy within an energy-intensive industrial context. By focusing on these factors, the
study responds to calls for greater empirical attention to the social dimensions of energy
transition in emerging economies and resource-dependent sectors.
This study makes several original contributions to the renewable energy transition
literature. First, it provides sector-specific empirical evidence from Zambia’s mining indus-
try, an energy-intensive sector that remains under-represented in transition studies despite
its strategic importance. Second, it moves beyond conceptual discussion by quantitatively
assessing and comparing the explanatory power of multiple social factors influencing solar
transition intentions. Third, socio-technical transition theory is empirically operationalised
by translating the social subsystem into measurable structural and people-centred variables
and testing their relationships using hierarchical regression. Finally, the study contributes
insights from an emerging economy context, addressing a literature gap that is predomi-
nantly dominated by evidence from developed energy markets.
2. Literature Review
Renewable energy systems based on locally available resources can enhance both
environmental sustainability and economic resilience [24]. In Zambia, high solar poten-
tial presents a clear opportunity to strengthen energy security and reduce reliance on
hydropower and imported fuels through decentralised generation systems [11]. How-
ever, evidence from energy transition research shows that the successful deployment of
renewable technologies depends not only on resource availability but also on the social and
institutional conditions that shape adoption decisions [5,16].
Recent studies emphasise that sustainability outcomes are strongly influenced by
institutional coordination, governance structures and supporting infrastructure, alongside
technological capability [25]. These factors are particularly salient in energy-intensive
sectors such as mining, where operational continuity, investment risk and system reliability
condition the feasibility of integrating solar energy into existing production systems. In
https://doi.org/10.3390/resources15010017
Resources 2026, 15, 17
3 of 24
such contexts, social mechanisms influence transition pathways by shaping regulatory
certainty, financial viability, organisational capacity and stakeholder alignment.
Guided by socio-technical transition theory [26], this study conceptualises solar energy
transition as the outcome of interactions between social, institutional and technical systems
rather than purely technological change. Within this framework, transition outcomes are
shaped by governance structures, actor behaviour, institutional coordination and learning
processes alongside technological capabilities. Accordingly, the study focuses on the social
subsystem of the mining energy system and operationalises two core components: a struc-
tural system, comprising policies, incentive systems and communication channels, and
a people-centred system, comprising training and skills development, and stakeholder en-
gagement and partnerships. Together, these five social factors capture the key institutional,
behavioural and coordination mechanisms through which renewable energy transition
intentions are enabled or constrained in energy-intensive sectors such as mining. To ensure
analytical relevance, the review prioritises recent literature on renewable energy transitions
in emerging-market and industrial contexts, supplemented by foundational socio-technical
studies that underpin the study’s theoretical framing.
2.1. Policies
Energy policy influences renewable energy transitions by shaping regulatory certainty,
investment expectations and institutional coordination [27]. Evidence from developed
contexts shows that renewable uptake is strongest where long-term policy objectives are
aligned with clear implementation responsibilities, as illustrated by Ireland’s National
Policy Statement on the Bioeconomy [24]. In Zambia, policies articulated under the Na-
tional Energy Policy (2019) and the Eighth National Development Plan (2022–2026) express
similar ambitions but remain weakly translated into enforceable and sector-specific im-
plementation mechanisms [28,29]. This gap between policy intent and operational clarity
weakens the credibility of regulatory signals required for long-term energy investment in
the mining sector.
Zambia’s renewable energy framework is underpinned by several legal and strategic
instruments, including the National Energy Policy (2019), Vision 2030, the Electricity Act
No. 11 of 2019, the Energy Regulation Act No. 12 of 2019 and the Renewable Energy Feed-in
Tariff (REFiT) introduced in 2017 [5,30,31]. While these instruments promote diversification
through solar, wind and biomass, empirical studies consistently find them insufficient to
stimulate large-scale industrial adoption [5,32–35]. Although the NEP targets renewables
supplying 40 per cent of total energy by 2030 [28], it lacks clear timelines, enforcement
mechanisms and mining-specific provisions addressing firm power supply, energy storage
and long-term power-purchase arrangements.
International experience shows that binding policy instruments, such as renewable
portfolio standards and enforceable compliance targets, reduce uncertainty and acceler-
ate adoption through predictable regulatory incentives [36,37]. By contrast, Zambia’s
framework relies largely on aspirational targets and administrative guidance, limiting
its influence on firm-level behaviour. Despite recent efforts to strengthen licensing and
enforcement [17,23,38], coordination gaps and limited support for system integration per-
sist. Overall, Zambia’s policy framework provides an important foundation for renewable
energy development but offers weak behavioural signals for mining firms considering
large-scale solar investment.
2.2. Incentive Systems
Financial incentives strongly influence renewable energy adoption by improving
project financial viability and reducing perceived investment risk [39,40]. This pattern is
https://doi.org/10.3390/resources15010017
Resources 2026, 15, 17
4 of 24
evident in Zambia’s mining sector, where access to tax reliefs, concessional financing and
other structured incentives remains a key driver of solar adoption. This highlights that
economic feasibility, rather than awareness alone, shapes the pace of renewable transition
in developing contexts. Instruments such as feed-in tariffs and net metering provide
predictable revenue streams, shorten payback periods and reduce investment uncertainty,
contributing to accelerated deployment in countries such as Germany, Spain and the
UK [5,41].
In Zambia, the Renewable Energy Feed-in Tariff (REFiT) introduced in 2017 offers guar-
anteed tariffs for projects up to 20 MW [31,42], supported by tax exemptions, zero import
duties on renewable equipment and recent green-budget measures, including green bonds
and electric vehicle incentives [43]. Programmes such as Scaling Solar and the Rural Elec-
trification Fund have expanded both utility-scale and off-grid renewable projects [44,45],
with support from international partners including the African Development Bank and
the European Union [5]. More recent initiatives, such as green bond guidelines and the
Green Compact with the UK, further signal growing institutional support for renewable
investment [17,46].
Despite these measures, most incentives remain oriented towards small-scale or rural
applications rather than large industrial users. The absence of long-term, sector-specific
financial instruments limits mining firms’ ability to invest in stable, large-scale renewable
systems capable of reducing exposure to power disruptions [5]. Expanding predictable and
industry-tailored incentive schemes would therefore reduce investment uncertainty and
support more consistent operational performance. As noted by Uhunamure et al. [39], well-
designed financial incentives act as enabling mechanisms that align economic feasibility
with renewable transition objectives in energy-intensive sectors such as mining.
2.3. Communication Channels
Effective communication channels influence solar energy transition by reducing infor-
mation asymmetries and improving confidence among industry and community actors.
Higher levels of knowledge and awareness have been shown to increase willingness to
pay for renewable energy and strengthen adoption intentions [40]. This highlights the im-
portance of clear, timely information flows within Zambia’s mining sector, where targeted
communication and training efforts can improve organisational readiness and acceptance of
solar technologies. In practice, weak communication between policymakers, implementers
and end-users has slowed renewable uptake in Zambia by limiting understanding of policy
instruments, technical options and associated benefits, thereby reducing confidence in new
technologies [47].
Within the mining sector, reliable data exchange systems further support coordination
and operational security. Secure communication protocols help maintain the integrity of
energy information networks and protect against unauthorised access [48], which is partic-
ularly important for energy planning in capital-intensive operations. At the community
level, communication channels also play a critical role in maintaining social licence by
clarifying project benefits, addressing concerns and managing expectations. Engagement
approaches that use local languages, trusted leaders and appropriate media platforms
can foster ownership and acceptance [5]. Without such mechanisms, renewable energy
initiatives risk misinformation, resistance and limited community support, particularly in
areas closely linked to mining activities.
2.4. Training and Skills Development
A major constraint to renewable energy adoption is limited training and skills develop-
ment, which affects solar energy transition by strengthening or constraining organisational
https://doi.org/10.3390/resources15010017
Resources 2026, 15, 17
5 of 24
and technical capacity to install, operate and maintain modern energy systems. Zambia
faces notable skills shortages in renewable and energy-efficient technologies, restricting the
mining sector’s ability to transition effectively [49,50]. Bridging these gaps is essential for
integrating renewable sources such as solar, wind, geothermal and biomass, which are pro-
jected to supply about 15 per cent of national energy use by 2030 [48]. Strengthening skills
not only supports energy transition but also contributes to economic growth, employment
creation and enterprise development along renewable value chains [51].
Training and skills development is particularly important in early-stage markets where
technical expertise and operational experience remain limited [5]. The National Energy Pol-
icy (2019) identifies institutional capacity enhancement as a priority and calls for increased
investment in research and innovation [28]. However, Zambia continues to experience
shortages of specialised professionals and practical renewable energy expertise [5,52].
While development partners such as UNDP have introduced training and advisory sup-
port through Global Environment Facility initiatives, these programmes remain modest
in scale [53]. Sustainable outcomes in the mining sector therefore depend on targeted
capacity-building mechanisms that enable firms to internalise technical knowledge, reduce
operational risk and sustain solar energy systems over the long term.
2.5. Stakeholder Engagement and Partnerships
Stakeholder engagement influences solar energy transition by enabling institutional
coordination, reducing implementation conflict and maintaining social licence for renew-
able energy projects. The National Energy Policy (2019) highlights coordinated stakeholder
participation as a key requirement for effective renewable energy implementation [28].
Talwar et al. [24] show that cooperative frameworks can significantly expand resource
availability, as demonstrated in Ireland where collaboration between farms, industries
and local authorities increased biomass supply. This insight is directly relevant to Zam-
bia’s mining sector, where partnerships between government agencies, mining companies
and surrounding communities can provide the coordination and legitimacy needed for
successful solar adoption.
In practice, weak engagement has slowed progress. Zambia’s energy governance
remains highly centralised, limiting local participation and constraining decentralised
solutions [20]. Cases such as Mokambo peri-urban in Mufulira illustrate how limited
consultation and poor awareness can delay renewable energy uptake [34]. Collaboration
gaps among implementers, researchers and funding agencies further restrict progress [5,34].
Effective engagement therefore requires structured outreach mechanisms that align gov-
ernment, industry and community actors around shared objectives. Public–private collab-
oration aligned with national transition policies can strengthen implementation [17,54],
although challenges persist due to fragmented planning, uneven technical capacity and
weak accountability mechanisms [55]. Regional experience suggests that multi-stakeholder
platforms linking government, financiers and civil society can improve inclusiveness and
coordination. In Zambia, such efforts depend on supportive regulatory guidance and
clearly defined governance responsibilities [56].
3. Materials and Methods
Guided by socio-technical transition theory [26], this study adopted a quantitative
research design to examine how social subsystems influence organisational intentions to
transition toward solar energy in Zambia’s mining sector. The analytical framework focused
on the social subsystem of the mining–energy system, distinguishing between structural
factors (policies, incentive systems and communication channels) and people-centred
factors (training and skills development, and stakeholder engagement and partnerships).

A questionnaire-based survey was used to collect structured data that allowed systematic
assessment of stakeholder perceptions across these dimensions.
3.1. Questionnaire Design and Measurement
The research instrument was a closed-ended questionnaire developed from estab-
lished empirical studies on renewable energy adoption and socio-technical transitions, and
adapted to reflect the institutional and operational context of Zambia’s mining sector [57].
The questionnaire comprised two sections: respondent demographics and measurement
items for the key social variables. Each construct was measured using multiple items to
capture its underlying dimensions: policies (5 items), incentive systems (5 items), com-
munication channels (4 items), training and skills development (4 items), stakeholder
engagement and partnerships (5 items), and transition intentions as the dependent vari-
able (4 items). Item wording was contextualised to reflect mining-sector decision-making,
regulatory exposure and energy reliability concerns.
Content validity was ensured through expert review by academics in energy policy
and construction management, as well as practitioners with experience in Zambia’s energy
and mining sectors. Minor revisions were made to improve clarity and contextual align-
ment. Responses were recorded using a five-point Likert scale ranging from 1 (strongly
disagree) to 5 (strongly agree). Full construct definitions and item wording are provided in
Supplementary Table S1 to enhance transparency and reproducibility.
3.2. Sampling and Data Collection
Respondents were purposively sampled to target professionals in technical, man-
agerial and strategic roles across mining companies, energy providers and regulatory
institutions. Snowball sampling was subsequently used to identify additional knowl-
edgeable participants [58]. Data were collected over a four-month period using an online
Google Form (Google LLC, Mountain View, CA, USA) distributed via email invitations and
follow-up reminders. In total, 192 valid responses were obtained.
The sample size was considered appropriate for capturing the diversity of Zambia’s
mining-energy stakeholder landscape, which is relatively concentrated and institution-
ally bounded. Zambia’s mining sector comprises a limited number of large-scale mining
firms, a small group of energy suppliers and a defined set of regulatory bodies rather than
a large population of homogeneous actors. The final sample reflects representation across
organisational types, ownership structures and experience levels, with respondents drawn
primarily from the Copperbelt and North-Western regions, where mining activity and
energy demand are most concentrated. The objective was therefore analytical representa-
tiveness rather than statistical generalisation, ensuring inclusion of informed actors across
the mining energy system.
3.3. Data Analysis, Validity and Reliability Assessment
Descriptive statistics were computed to summarise central tendency and dispersion
across variables. All statistical analyses were conducted using IBM SPSS Statistics for
Windows, Version 27.0 (IBM Corp., Armonk, NY, USA). Internal consistency reliability
was assessed using Cronbach’s alpha, with values of 0.60 and above accepted given the
exploratory nature of the study. Construct validity was evaluated using Exploratory Factor
Analysis (EFA), supported by the Kaiser–Meyer–Olkin (KMO) measure and Bartlett’s Test
of Sphericity, which confirmed the suitability of the data for factor analysis.
Hierarchical regression analysis was employed to examine the incremental explanatory
power of social factors on transition intentions. Variables were entered in a theory-driven
sequence informed by socio-technical theory. Control variables were entered first, followed

3.4. Control Variables
Control variables were selected based on prior literature on energy transition and
organisational innovation, which identifies firm size, financial capacity and organisational
maturity as background factors influencing technology adoption. Enterprise size, num-
ber of employees, annual turnover and organisational tenure were therefore included to
account for baseline differences in capacity and structural positioning. These variables
are commonly used in innovation and energy transition studies and allowed the analysis
to isolate the explanatory power of social and institutional factors beyond differences
in organisational scale and maturity. Other potential controls, such as corporate carbon
footprint, were excluded due to limited availability of consistent emissions data and the
perception-based nature of the survey instrument.
4. Results
This section presents the findings from the quantitative analysis of data collected from
mining sector respondents on the social factors influencing solar energy transitioning (SET)
in Zambia. The analysis integrates results from descriptive statistics, factor loading and
reliability tests, and hierarchical regression models. These analyses provide an understand-
ing of how policies, incentives, communication, training, and stakeholder engagement
influence renewable energy readiness within the mining industry.
4.1. Demographic Descriptive Statistics
The following section presents the demographic characteristics of the respondents,
providing context for interpreting the study’s findings.
Table 1 summarises the demographic characteristics of 192 respondents representing
mining companies, energy firms, and other related stakeholders. Engineers made up
the largest group (38.5%), followed by managers (18.8%) and executive directors (7.3%),
showing that most respondents were involved in technical and operational roles related to
energy and production. Approximately 35.4% of participants had worked in their organisa-
tions for over ten years, indicating strong institutional experience. In terms of ownership,
33.3% of respondents were from state-owned institutions, 31.3% from privately owned
entities, 13.5% from publicly listed firms, and 13.5% from joint ventures. Mining compa-
nies accounted for 43.8% of the sample, followed by government bodies (27.1%), energy
companies (14.6%), and NGOs (6.3%). The composition of respondents across different

4.2. Kruskal–Wallis
The results of the Kruskal–Wallis test are summarised in Table 2. A Kruskal–Wallis
test was conducted to examine whether future intentions to transition differed across three
respondent groups: mining companies, energy suppliers and other mining stakeholders.
This nonparametric test provides a suitable alternative to one-way ANOVA when compar-
ing three or more independent groups [59]. The analysis used the intention scores as the
dependent variable.

4.3. Descriptive Statistics
Descriptive statistics were used to summarise the main patterns in the data and
provide an overview of respondents’ perceptions. Measures such as means, standard
deviations, skewness and kurtosis helped show central tendencies, variability and the
distribution shape. These results offered a basic understanding of the responses before
moving to more advanced analyses.
Table 3 summarises construct-level descriptive statistics for the social variables in-
fluencing solar energy transition in Zambia’s mining sector. Detailed item-level statistics
are provided in Supplementary Table S2 to ensure transparency. Mean values across the
five constructs range from approximately 2.96 to 3.28, indicating moderate agreement and
emerging readiness for solar adoption.

Training and skills development recorded the highest mean (Mean = 3.28, SD = 0.61),
highlighting recognition of capacity as a key enabler, although variability suggests uneven
skills and coordination. Stakeholder engagement and partnerships also showed a positive
mean (Mean = 3.22, SD = 0.48), underscoring the perceived importance of collaboration
in energy-intensive sectors. Policy frameworks and incentive systems exhibited similar
moderate means (Policies: Mean = 3.21, SD = 0.24; Incentives: Mean = 3.03, SD = 0.30),
reflecting awareness of policy and financial instruments alongside concerns about effec-
tiveness and access. Communication channels recorded the lowest mean (Mean = 2.96,
SD = 0.21), pointing to persistent information and coordination gaps. Overall, the results
indicate partial institutional readiness for solar energy transition, constrained by imple-
mentation and communication challenges. These descriptive patterns provide context for
the subsequent regression analysis examining the relative influence of social factors on
transition intentions.
4.4. Validity and Reliability
Given the exploratory nature of this study and the limited availability of context-
specific measurement scales for solar energy transitioning in Zambia’s mining sector,
Exploratory Factor Analysis (EFA) was employed to assess construct validity [60]. EFA
is well suited to emerging research contexts, particularly in developing-country settings,
where underlying factor structures require empirical identification. Sampling adequacy was
evaluated using the Kaiser–Meyer–Olkin (KMO) measure and Bartlett’s Test of Sphericity,
both of which confirmed the suitability of the data for factor analysis. Internal consistency
reliability was assessed using Cronbach’s alpha, with all constructs exceeding the accepted
threshold of 0.70, indicating satisfactory reliability. These validity and reliability assess-
ments were complemented by regression diagnostics to confirm that statistical assumptions

these procedures strengthen the robustness and credibility of the findings in the absence of

The KMO value of 0.785 indicated that the sample size was adequate for factor analysis
and exceeded the recommended minimum threshold of 0.50, demonstrating strong sam-
pling adequacy [1,2]. This value also surpassed the 0.70 benchmark commonly associated
with good construct validity, confirming that the correlation patterns among variables
were appropriate for dimension reduction. Bartlett’s Test of Sphericity was statistically
significant (χ2 = 1280.592, df = 231, p < 0.001), suggesting that the correlation matrix was
not an identity matrix and that the variables shared sufficient common variance to justify
factor extraction. Together, the KMO results, Bartlett’s test outcome, and internal reliability
coefficients confirm that the questionnaire was both statistically valid and reliable, support-
ing its suitability for examining the social factors influencing solar energy transitioning in
the mining sector.

4.8. Hierarchical Regression
Hierarchical regression analysis was employed to assess how social subsystem vari-
ables influence intentions to transition to solar energy after accounting for basic organi-
sational characteristics. The regression followed a theory-driven sequence informed by
socio-technical theory. Model 1 included organisational control variables (firm size, em-
ployee numbers, turnover, and tenure) to account for baseline differences in organisational
capacity. Model 2 introduced the structural and enabling social factors; policies, communi-
cation channels, and training and skills development which reflect the institutional and
informational context shaping transition decisions. Model 3 subsequently added the more
agency-driven social factors, namely incentive systems and stakeholder engagement and
partnerships, to assess their incremental explanatory power beyond the weaker structural
variables. The inclusion of the social subsystem variables increased the explained variance
in transition intentions from R2 = 0.016 to R2 = 0.201 (∆R2 = 0.185, p < 0.01). Notably, stake-
holder engagement emerged as the strongest contributor, indicating that participatory and
collaborative mechanisms exert a greater influence on transition intentions after control-
ling for organisational characteristics and broader structural conditions. This sequencing
allowed for a clear assessment of how explanatory power improved at each stage through
changes in R2 and associated F-change values.
To assess the robustness of the empirical findings, several complementary diagnostic
and validation procedures were applied. Multicollinearity was examined using variance
inflation factors (VIF), with all values remaining below the conservative threshold of 3,
indicating that intercorrelations among predictors did not distort coefficient estimates.
Given the perceptual and cross-sectional nature of the survey data, common method
bias was assessed using Harman’s single-factor test, which showed that no single factor
accounted for the majority of variance, suggesting that common method effects were
unlikely to drive the results. In addition, the stability of the regression estimates was
examined through alternative model specifications, including reordering of predictor
blocks and exclusion of non-significant variables, with the substantive pattern of results
remaining unchanged. These checks increase confidence that the observed dominance of
stakeholder engagement and incentive systems reflects genuine structural relationships
rather than artefacts of model specification or measurement design. Table 8 presents
the hierarchical regression results for the predictors of solar energy transition intentions,
showing the incremental contribution of control variables and social subsystem factors
across the three models.

ables (tenure, number of employees, annual turnover and organisation size). Model 1 ex-

5. Discussion
The study examined the social factors influencing solar energy transition (SET) in
Zambia’s mining sector. Drawing on socio-technical theory, it explored how the inter-
action between social, institutional, and technical systems shapes the mining industry’s
readiness for renewable energy adoption. The discussion links the empirical results to
the reviewed literature and interprets their meaning within the wider context of Zambia’s
energy transition efforts.
5.1. Policies-Related Findings
The findings show that although respondents acknowledged the existence of policies
supporting renewable energy, these policies did not significantly predict solar adoption
intentions. This aligns with observations by Mhango [5] and Chitandula et al. [11], who
https://doi.org/10.3390/resources15010017
Resources 2026, 15, 17
16 of 24
argue that Zambia’s policy environment is well articulated but weak in delivery. Instru-
ments such as the National Energy Policy (2019), the Electricity Act No. 11 of 2019 and the
Renewable Energy Feed-in Tariff (2017) signal government commitment, yet they lack the
operational clarity required to drive meaningful uptake within the mining sector [28,31].
Pegels [32] similarly notes that policy frameworks in developing-country contexts often
underperform when enforcement, accountability and resource allocation mechanisms are
insufficient, while Wojnarowska et al. [25] emphasise the importance of evidence-based
assessment in guiding effective sustainability transitions.
Beyond their descriptive intent, Zambia’s national energy policies exhibit several struc-
tural contradictions that constrain effective implementation within the mining sector. While
policy instruments promote renewable energy diversification, they are not consistently
supported by sector-specific operational guidelines, coordinated institutional responsi-
bilities or clearly defined enforcement mechanisms. This disconnect creates uncertainty
for mining firms that require predictable regulatory signals to support long-term energy
investment decisions. As a result, policies function more as aspirational frameworks than
as actionable transition tools, limiting their influence on firm-level behaviour despite their
stated objectives.
The weaker predictive effect of policy variables reflects a clear distinction between
the existence of policy frameworks and their perceived effectiveness in practice. From
a socio-technical perspective, policies influence transition behaviour primarily by shaping
regulatory credibility, investment certainty and coordination across actors; where enforce-
ment mechanisms, sector-specific guidance and predictable implementation pathways
are weak, policy frameworks generate limited behavioural signals for firm-level decision-
making. In the mining sector, where energy decisions are shaped by operational risk, cost
certainty and supply reliability, policies that lack enforceable instruments, sector-specific
guidance, predictable timelines and financial integration provide weak behavioural sig-
nals. Consequently, while Zambia’s policy instruments establish a necessary foundation
for renewable energy growth, they do not yet deliver the credibility or implementation
pathways required to influence firm-level transition intentions or reduce energy-related
operational vulnerabilities.
5.2. Incentive Systems-Related Findings
Incentive systems exhibited a positive and statistically significant influence on solar
energy transition intentions, underscoring the central role of financial viability in shaping
corporate energy decisions. This finding aligns with studies by Kılıç and Kekezoglu [41]
and Chitandula et al. [17], which highlight the importance of financial instruments such
as tax exemptions, feed-in tariffs and concessional loans in expanding renewable energy
adoption. However, the relatively modest mean scores observed in this study suggest
that access to such incentives in Zambia remains limited or administratively complex,
particularly for large industrial users. Previous research similarly indicates that existing
incentive structures are more oriented towards small-scale or off-grid applications than
towards energy-intensive sectors such as mining [5,11].
While incentive-based renewable energy policies in countries such as Germany and
Spain have been effective in accelerating solar deployment, their direct replication in Zam-
bia is constrained by differences in institutional capacity, market structure and financial
depth. European incentive regimes operate within stable regulatory environments, liber-
alised electricity markets, mature grid infrastructure and well-developed green finance
ecosystems. In contrast, Zambia’s energy sector is characterised by a vertically structured
electricity market, limited fiscal space, evolving regulatory enforcement and constrained
access to long-term finance. Consequently, the portability of international experience lies
https://doi.org/10.3390/resources15010017
Resources 2026, 15, 17
17 of 24
less in replicating specific subsidy levels or tariff designs, and more in adapting core prin-
ciples such as policy credibility, administrative simplicity and investor certainty to local
institutional realities.
From an engineering and financial perspective, the relevance of incentive systems is
further reinforced by global declines in photovoltaic technology costs and the emergence of
green financing instruments. Over the past decade, falling module prices, improved system
efficiencies and advances in energy storage have reduced the capital intensity of solar
projects, making them increasingly viable for industrial applications. However, the ability
of mining firms to capitalise on these trends depends on access to appropriate financial
mechanisms, including concessional loans, blended finance, green bonds and structured
power-purchase agreements. In Zambia, the limited penetration of such instruments
constrains the translation of technological cost reductions into realised investment decisions,
highlighting the importance of aligning financial innovation with institutional incentives
and stakeholder coordination.
Insufficient or poorly targeted incentives limit the ability of mining companies to
invest in stable and affordable energy systems, with direct implications for production
reliability and operational costs. The findings therefore suggest that expanding incentive
schemes tailored to industrial energy needs, alongside simplifying access procedures,
would support wider renewable energy uptake. Well-designed financial instruments can
act as enabling conditions that align social and technical subsystems, facilitate innovation
diffusion across mining operations and strengthen long-term sector performance.
5.3. Communication Channels-Related Findings
Communication channels recorded low mean values and non-significant regression
results, indicating continuing weaknesses in information flow between government, reg-
ulators and industry. Poor communication has previously been identified as a barrier to
effective energy policy implementation in Zambia [16,47]. Although the construct showed
high reliability, its weak statistical influence suggests that information-sharing mechanisms
are present but not operating effectively. Limited awareness of incentives, unclear technical
guidelines and irregular updates on national targets reduce confidence in the transition
process and make coordinated planning more difficult.
Communication acts as a link between policy design and the practical uptake of new
technologies. Strengthening this link through transparent data systems, structured consul-
tations and consistent public reporting would support better decision-making, improve
alignment across organisations and help reduce disruptions associated with energy uncer-
tainty. Clearer and more efficient communication would therefore create conditions that
encourage smoother adoption of solar energy within mining operations.
5.4. Training and Skills Development-Related Findings
Training and skills development showed no significant influence on solar energy
transition in this study. Respondents noted that while some technical capability exists,
structured training, coordinated learning and practical exposure remain limited. This
aligns with earlier work highlighting gaps in human capital and institutional capacity as
major barriers to renewable energy adoption in Zambia [2,5]. Limited access to regular
training and skills development affects how confidently organisations can prepare for new
technologies and increases uncertainty when planning transition activities.
Strengthening training systems would improve familiarity with solar technologies,
enhance problem-solving capacity and support more informed decision-making within
mining operations. Better-skilled personnel are also likely to manage energy systems more
efficiently, reduce operational disruptions and contribute to more stable and reliable pro-
https://doi.org/10.3390/resources15010017

duction. Targeted up skilling, industry academic partnerships and continuous professional
development would therefore help create conditions that support a smoother and more
sustainable shift to solar energy.
5.5. Stakeholder Engagement and Partnerships-Related Findings
Stakeholder engagement and partnerships emerged as the strongest and most con-
sistent predictor of solar energy transition intentions, underscoring the central role of
coordination and collective action in enabling renewable energy adoption. This finding
aligns with earlier studies showing that effective collaboration among government agencies,
mining companies, financiers and communities is critical for overcoming institutional frag-
mentation and implementation barriers in energy transitions [5,11,22,27]. Through shared
decision-making, information exchange and risk distribution, engagement mechanisms
reduce uncertainty and strengthen confidence in new energy investments.
Evidence from South Africa [51] and Zambia further indicates that renewable energy
uptake is shaped not only by technical capacity but also by social and behavioural condi-
tions. Policy support, affordability and engagement interact to influence organisational
readiness, particularly in energy-intensive sectors such as mining. As noted by Nyokabi
et al. [53], cooperation among diverse actors enhances accountability, resource mobilisation
and knowledge transfer, while partnerships with research institutions and technology
providers facilitate solutions aligned with operational mining requirements [52]. In Zambia,
weak engagement structures have limited project scalability and slowed implementation,
reflecting gaps in coordination rather than a lack of technological potential.
The strong predictive effect of stakeholder engagement observed in this study re-
inforces the socio-technical perspective that energy transitions advance most effectively
when actor networks are aligned around shared objectives and coordinated governance
arrangements. By enabling trust, coordination and institutional learning, stakeholder part-
nerships function as a key transmission channel through which social conditions translate
into concrete transition intentions within the mining sector.
5.6. Broader Implications
The findings indicate that progress toward renewable energy transition in Zambia’s
mining sector is shaped primarily by social conditions, which remain uneven across in-
stitutions. Policy frameworks alone are insufficient without credible incentives, effective
communication and coordinated stakeholder engagement. Financial viability and collabo-
rative arrangements exert greater influence on transition outcomes than broad regulatory
statements or stand-alone training initiatives.
Although grounded in Zambia’s mining sector, the findings are analytically transfer-
able to other African mining contexts with similar energy profiles. Mining industries across
the continent are characterised by high energy intensity, exposure to supply disruptions and
capital-intensive production systems. In such settings, incentive credibility, institutional
trust and stakeholder coordination are likely to play a decisive role in shaping renewable
energy adoption, although the relative influence of policy variables may increase where
regulatory enforcement is stronger.
The results also hold relevance for other energy-intensive industries in Zambia, such
as manufacturing, where firms similarly depend on reliable and affordable electricity.
While adoption pathways may vary with firm size, ownership and access to finance,
the importance of credible incentives, clear information flows and coordinated engage-
ment remains consistent. This suggests that the social mechanisms identified in this
study are not sector-specific but require contextual adaptation to different operational and
regulatory environments.

Beyond organisational and institutional factors, broader acceptance by mining com-
munities and labour groups is also critical to the feasibility and durability of renewable
energy projects. Community trust, perceptions of fairness, employment impacts and local
participation influence the social licence to operate, particularly where mining activities are
closely embedded within surrounding communities. Although these dynamics were not ex-
amined directly, they interact closely with organisational decision-making and stakeholder
engagement processes.
From a policy perspective, the findings underscore the need for renewable energy
governance that prioritises practical implementation over policy expansion. Clearer sector-
specific guidance, transparent incentive access, improved information systems and targeted
capacity-building would support more consistent adoption. Strengthened partnerships
among government, industry, research institutions and communities can further reduce
coordination gaps, improve alignment and create more stable conditions for energy invest-
ment in the mining sector.
5.7. Research Limitations and Future Research Directions
This study is based on cross-sectional survey data and therefore captures stakeholder
perceptions at a single point in time. As a result, the analysis cannot establish causal
relationships between social factors and solar energy transition intentions, nor account
for how these relationships may evolve as policy frameworks, market conditions and
organisational practices change. Future research could address this limitation through
longitudinal designs that track transition dynamics over time.
The study also relies on self-reported data collected using a single survey instrument,
which introduces the potential for common method bias. While established statistical
procedures were applied to assess reliability and validity, future studies could strengthen
robustness by combining survey data with objective indicators, documentary analysis or
multiple data sources.
In addition, the analysis focuses on perceived policy effectiveness and organisational-
level social drivers, without incorporating qualitative interviews or detailed policy text
analysis. Further research could complement these findings through in-depth qualita-
tive engagement with policymakers, regulators and industry stakeholders, as well as
systematic examination of policy instruments, to better understand institutional contradic-
tions, implementation dynamics and enforcement mechanisms within Zambia’s energy
governance framework.
The study also does not explicitly examine social acceptance among mining communi-
ties and labour groups, which represents an important dimension of sustainable energy
transitions. Issues such as community trust, employment impacts and local participation
can significantly influence the social licence to operate and warrant focused investigation
in future research.
Finally, while the findings are analytically transferable, the study is context-specific
to Zambia’s mining sector. Comparative research across Sub-Saharan African mining
economies with differing regulatory and institutional environments, as well as across
other energy-intensive industries such as manufacturing, would help assess the broader
applicability of the social mechanisms identified. Such work would provide deeper insight
into the conditions that support reliable energy provision and strengthen the operational
environment for sustainable energy transitions in industrial sectors.
6. Conclusions
This study examined the social factors shaping solar energy transition in Zambia’s
mining sector, focusing on policies, incentive systems, communication channels, training
https://doi.org/10.3390/resources15010017
Resources 2026, 15, 17
20 of 24
and skills development, and stakeholder engagement and partnerships. The findings
indicate that although mining stakeholders recognise persistent energy challenges, progress
towards solar adoption remains constrained by weak policy implementation, limited access
to incentives, inadequate information flows and insufficient structured training. Among the
five factors analysed, stakeholder engagement and incentive systems exerted the strongest
influence on transition intentions, while policies, communication and training showed
limited statistical significance. These results reinforce existing evidence highlighting the
central role of collaboration, regulatory credibility and accessible financial support in
advancing renewable energy adoption within energy-intensive industries [5,11,27].
The differentiated effects observed across social factors suggest that effective policy
responses must move beyond broad regulatory statements. The strong influence of incen-
tive systems indicates that government interventions should prioritise predictable, sector-
specific financial instruments for mining operations, including streamlined tax reliefs, con-
cessional financing and bankable power-purchase arrangements. Similarly, the consistent
significance of stakeholder engagement underscores the importance of formalised coordi-
nation platforms linking government agencies, mining firms, energy companies, financiers
and host communities to reduce uncertainty and improve implementation outcomes.
By contrast, the weak predictive effect of policy frameworks suggests that policy
expansion alone is unlikely to accelerate transition. Greater emphasis is required on
implementation credibility through enforceable timelines, sector-specific guidance and
improved alignment between energy, mining and fiscal institutions. Communication and
training initiatives, while necessary, are most effective when integrated with incentive
delivery and engagement structures that translate awareness into investment decisions.
Overall, the findings indicate that solar energy transition in Zambia’s mining sector depends
less on policy proliferation and more on targeted incentives, coordinated governance and
credible implementation mechanisms aligned with industrial realities.
Building on the empirical results, more targeted and actor-specific actions are required
to advance transition. For government institutions, optimising incentive systems involves
simplifying access procedures, aligning instruments with the needs of large industrial
energy users and providing stable, long-term financial mechanisms that reduce investment
uncertainty. Regulatory agencies should strengthen inter-institutional coordination to
ensure that policy intentions translate into enforceable, sector-relevant implementation
pathways. For mining enterprises, the results highlight the importance of structured
stakeholder engagement through partnerships with energy providers, financiers, research
institutions and local authorities to share risks, mobilise expertise and support project
delivery. Emerging solar pilot initiatives in mining regions illustrate how coordinated
collaboration can convert declining solar technology costs into operational energy solutions.
At the community level, early and transparent engagement, local participation and effective
information sharing remain essential for building trust, addressing labour concerns and
maintaining social licence to operate. Collectively, these actions demonstrate how incentive
systems and stakeholder participation can be operationalised in practice, consistent with
the social factors shown to most strongly influence transition intentions.
